title: Principles of Biochemistry
abstract:
the miner's doctor: "The salary was $275
a month, on call twenty-four hours ev¬
ery day with office hours from 7:00 to
12:00, 1:00 to 5:00, and 6:00 to 9:00 ex¬
cept Sunday. After one year, one week
vacation without pay."
Undated
black-and-white
photo¬
graphs from the West Virginia State
Archives of dusty, determined miners
are strategically placed and complement
the text. Both portray typical pre-World
War II coal towns. The air is hazy with
gray smog. Rows of small company-
owned frame rental houses and railroad
tracks terrace graded hills reforested
with tipples and cranes.
Photos and first-person accounts give
authenticity
to
Frazier's
history,
sprinkled with speculation, verbiage, and
local mountain idiom. Not all company
doctors were competent, he writes, "But
I believe it is safe to say that the ma¬
jority were conscientious people ofcour¬
age and great endurance who did the
best they could for their patients under
conditions that would try the soul of
today's physicians."
Contributors recall with unwarranted
fondness and joy a life made hazardous
by the threat of now rare but then un¬
beatable infectious diseases and inevi¬
table accidents. While describing unsani¬
taryliving conditions and gruesome mine
disasters, bitter strikes, and union wars,
Frazier notes, "With few exceptions,
those such as myself who grew up in
those communities write of their memo¬
ries with a great deal of affection and
pleasure." The passage of time softens
the memories of growing up in misery
and poverty. One person wrote, "Some
ofthe happiest days ofmy life were spent
in the coal camp."
West Virginia's proximity to popula¬
tion centers in the East and Midwest,
combined with its vast supply of coal,
petroleum, and natural gas, should have
brought prosperity to its citizens during
the last 100 years. Instead, after the
Civil War, coal companies purchased for
a pittance broad form deeds—perpetual
leases that granted the right to build
roads, bore, scrape, and destroy to get
to minerals beneath the surface. Huge
profits went to absentee owners, and
the original land owners were left with
ruined farms and a meager royalty.
A feudal system grew in which rental
housing, education, and health care were
alldependent onemploymentinthe coal
mines. Miners were often paid with
"scrip" redeemable for goods only at the
company general store. Attempts to
unionize the workers set off coal wars,
barely remembered outside of Appala-
chia, in which the politicians and law
officers were aligned to thwart the min¬
ers. Union organizing was virtually il-
legal, and organizers were crushed by
legal machinery.
Periodically government commissions
would investigate conditions in the min¬
ing communities and recommend legis¬
lation to improve "the miner's lot." Sani¬
tation standards, child labor restrictions,
and mine safety regulations were rewrit¬
ten but rarely enforced. Expectations of
improved safety and health were unful¬
filled until mechanization changed min¬
ing methods and decreased the numbers
of workers. Cave-ins, explosions, and
black lung disease still threaten present-
day miners.
Frazier is an allergist from Asheville,
NC, and the son of a coal company doc¬
tor who practiced in McDowell County.
This book is a significant departure from
his many others, which have been mainly
texts intended to educate a lay audience
about allergies. He also wrote with
F. K. Brown Insects and Allergy and
What to Do About Them (University of
Oklahoma Press, 1980), in the
same
folksy style as Miners and Medicine.
Natives of Appalachia or those with
an interest in regional American medi¬
cal history will enjoy this informative,
colloquial volume.
Paul A. Fiore, MD
Morehead Memorial Hospital
Eden, NC
Biochemistry
Principles of Biochemistry, by Albert L.Lehninger,
David L. Nelson, and Michael M. Cox, 2nd ed,
1013+ pp, with illus, $65.95, ISBN 0-87901-500-4,
New York, NY, Worth Publishers Inc, 1993.
One cannot begin to review Principles
of Biochemistry without reflecting on
its history. Twenty-three years ago the
late Professor Albert L. Lehninger of
The Johns Hopkins University authored
Biochemistry. It was the first biochem-
istry textbook; it emphasized readabil-
ity, student engagement, comprehen-
siveness without overbearing inclusive-
ness; and it even had innovative pro-
duction, with wide margins that were
most often (but not always) the home of
careful illustrations. Students and teach-
ers alike embraced Biochemistry for
bringing the chemistry ofbiological sys-
tems and the molecular basis of life into
the domains ofordinary understandabil-
ity and educational importance at both
undergraduate and graduate levels. In
1975 an even better second edition ap-
peared.
In 1982 Lehninger wrote a new text
called Principles ofBiochemistry. Prin-
ciples followed the general pattern of
Biochemistry, but its focus and format
were
somehow different. Lehninger
wrote in the preface that "the time has
come when a single biochemistry text-
book cannot be all things to all students"
and that biochemistry "has become the
lingua franca of the life sciences [in pro¬
viding] challenging glimpses of how liv¬
ing organisms solve some of the most
fundamental chemical and physical prob¬
lems." Lehninger was the first to state
so succinctly that "a knowledge of bio¬
chemistry is useful for all well-informed
citizens, whatever their calling." Prin¬
ciples, along with Biochemistry, engen¬
dered much competition in textbooks
and much enthusiasm for learning how
life processes are directed by the inter¬
actions and transformations of biologi¬
cal molecules. Principles was designed
particularly for college students expe¬
riencing their first formal course in bio¬
chemistry.
'"aknowledge ofbiochemistry is use¬
ful for all well-informed citizens,
whatever their calling.'"
Some 11 years later, we are presented
with the second edition of Principles of
Biochemistry. Professors Nelson and
Cox, both of the University of Wiscon¬
sin, have rewritten, reworked, reformat¬
ted, and updated the original Principles
since Professor Lehninger's untimely
death. The Lehninger influence remains.
The new Principles of Biochemistry is
remarkable for its clarity, interest, and
production quality. Like its predeces¬
sor, it is divided into four parts. The
section in the first edition called "Some
Aspects ofHuman Biochemistry" (based
in part on Lehninger's interest in nu¬
trition) has disappeared, however, and
"Biomolecules"
has
been split
into
"Foundations
of Biochemistry"
and
"Structure and Catalysis." Multichap-
ter sections on "Bioenergetics and Me¬
tabolism" (with material on metabolic
integration and regulation) and "Infor¬
mation Pathways" (essentially molecu¬
lar biology) remain. All in all, the basic
organization of the text is unchanged
and is both comfortable and sound.
Principles is roughly the same length
as the original (about 1000 pages). Each
of its 28 chapters lists a goodly number
of excellent and sometimes very recent
references (to both reviews and seminal
publications), and each provides agoodly
number of problems (with abbreviated
answers) that
span broad
areas of
thought as well as quantitative manipu¬
lation. The index is comprehensive and
easy to use, and the illustrations are
numerous and helpful (even if occasion¬
ally they seem to be a little too compli¬
cated or pretty). There is a welcome
chapter on the importance of water in
living systems and a new chapter on
recombinant DNA technology. Prin-
 ciples, like all of its Lehninger-derived
predecessors, is at its best on the sub¬
jects of bioenergetics and metabolism.
With well-composed text, good illustra¬
tions, and a sprinkling of both mecha¬
nisms and cellular contexts, this section
(the largest in the book) manages to
present a huge amount of material in an
interesting, effective, and enjoyable way.
What is most special about Principles
is that it explains and teaches. It helps
to organize material in a student's mind
and helps the student to learn how to
think about biochemical processes and
what they mean. One does not often see
a textbook that seeks to bring students
into an entire field (in contrast to texts
that seek simply to present material),
and it is even less often that a textbook
accomplishes this goal as well as Prin¬
ciples. The weakness in Principles is
actually that ofthe first edition and con¬
cerns molecular biology. "Information
Pathways" represents about 22% of the
textbook (actually up from 16% in the
first edition) but, for example, devotes
only about eight pages to gene regula¬
tion in eukaryotes. One might have
wished for considerably more. Never¬
theless, a "single biochemistry textbook
cannot be all things to all students," other
texts and courses already cover related
material in well-conceived ways, and
Principles does a remarkably good job
in this area, as well as in others, ofteach¬
ing rather than telling.
Principles is a welcome reentrant into
the ever-expanding arena of textbooks
on general biochemistry. Professors Nel¬
son and Cox have done a remarkable
job, and their efforts will undoubtedly
have a considerable impact on the teach¬
ing of biochemistry to college students
ofboth biology and chemistry. The work
is also suitable for other beginning
courses and should be seriously consid¬
ered by teachers of medical school bio¬
chemistry. There are few. so-called clini¬
cal correlations here, but biochemistry
is biochemistry, and reading Lehninger,
Nelson, and Cox is certainly a very good
and very interesting way to learn it.
Howard S. Tager, PhD
The University of Chicago (Ill)
Autonomie Disorders
Clinical Autonomic Disorders: Evaluation and
Management, edited by Philip A. Low, 800 pp, with
illus, $165, ISBN 0-316-53390-4, Boston, Mass, Little,
Brown & Co, 1993.
Clinical Autonomic Disorders
was
compiled as a comprehensive summary
of current knowledge about the diagno-
sis and treatment of autonomic dysfunc-
tion, with an emphasis on disorders of
peripheral nerves. On the whole, Dr Low
has fulfilled that ambition. Although the
book clearly reflects the author's own
special interests, both as a clinician deal-
ing with autonomic neuropathies and as
founder of an autonomic laboratory at
the Mayo Clinic, its 800 pages and 56
chapters present a broad view of auto-
nomic function and failure. It is reason-
ably up-to-date, references in most chap-
ters extending to 1991.
The massive text is organized into
three major sections: basic science, clini-
cal evaluation, and clinical disorders. This
plan inevitably leads to repetition and
overlap, but, if the book is used as a
source of individual short monographs,
this may not be a problem. Careful at-
tention by the editor has ensured good
consistency among the authors, so that
there are fewer internal contradictions
than usual in a collection this size. He
has also sprinkled many helpful cross-
references throughout the text. On the
average, the 56 monographs are solid
and informative, and some offer infor¬
mation difficult to find elsewhere. Dr
Low, for example, details his own ex¬
perience in setting up the autonomie
laboratory at Mayo, including specific
testing procedures used there and even
the commercial sources ofhis equipment.
Although two thirds ofthe 57 authors
are Americans, the editor has drawn
from Europe, the United Kingdom, Aus¬
tralia, Canada, and Japan for expertise
and has tapped experts in dermatology,
anesthesiology, gastroenterology, endo¬
crinology, and psychology. This has pro¬
duced some interesting and sometimes
imaginative offerings, for example, Dr
Barry Arnason's chapter on the sym¬
pathetic nervous system and the immune
response. It also leaves some peculiar
gaps and emphases. The fine chapter on
"Syncope, Mechanisms, Approaches, and
Management" gives an excellent sum¬
mary of the mechanisms of vasovagal
syncope and continues with cogent ex¬
planations of newer cardiac tests that
will be valuable to the clinical neurolo¬
gist. But many neurologists (and other
physicians) would not agree that "ca¬
rotid sinus massage should be a man¬
datory part of the evaluation of any pa¬
tient with syncope" and would find the
suggested treatments of simple faints
excessive.
Other omissions are glaring. Three
chapters are devoted to sexual function
and dysfunction, including two on male
pathology, while female sexuality is "cov¬
ered" in a single paragraph. Chapters
on regulation of systemic blood pres¬
sure mainly ignore regulation of cere¬
bral perfusion, and I found no mention
of local mechanisms of vascular control
("nitric oxide" and "endothelial relaxing
factor" are not in the index), nor is there
anything more than a passing reference
to the role of cardiac atrial receptors
and atrial peptides in blood pressure
control. On the other hand, the discus¬
sion of autonomie failure and the ac¬
quired immunodeficiency syndrome and
that on postprandial hypotension will
fill a clinical gap for many neurologists.
The book has been nicely produced,
and, although bulky, it is easy to read
because of a clear typeface, sturdy pa¬
per, and well-organized section head¬
ings. The illustrations, particularly the
line drawings in the scientific section,
are large and exceptionally crisp. Un¬
fortunately for a book whose size and
format suggest use as a reference, the
index is less successful (no entries for
"lymphocytes" despite Dr. Arnason's
chapter, none for "urinary incontinence"
or "appetite").
As the author himselfdeclares, this is
a clinical book and neither claims to be
nor is an in-depth text on the basic sci¬
ence of autonomie function. The book
will be useful for clinical neurologists
and primary care physicians with a spe¬
cial interest in autonomie problems.
Donna C. Bergen, MD
Rush-Presbyterian-St Luke's
Medical Center
Chicago, Ill
Software
Portable Information
Digital Book System, by Franklin Electronic Pub-
lishers,
1 hand-held, 5\m=x\3.25\m=x\0.5-in16-bit com-
puter, stores 2 digital books simultaneously, in-
cludes Merriam-Webster's Dictionary Plus dictio-
nary and thesaurus and Word Games, $199, with
ROM cards for The Medical Letter Handbook of
Adverse Drug Interactions Digital Book ($99.95),
Pocket PDR Physicians `Desk Reference 1993 Digi-
tal Book ($129.95), and Washington University
Manual of Medical Therapeutics 27th ed Digital
Book ($99.95), Mount Holly, NJ, Franklin Electronic
Publishers Inc, 1993.
As the information explosion of the
1990s rolls on, compactness and port-
ability have become important in de-
signing systems for storing and access-
ing data. The Franklin Digital Book
System represents a technological mar-
vel, which allows entire books to be
stored on a matchbox-sized cartridge
(2.25\m=x\1.25\m=x\0.25in)
and played
or
viewed on an index-card-sized computer
(5\m=x\3.25\m=x\0.5in). The unit weighs only
4.6 oz and runs on four CR2032 batter-
ies. Up to 45 megabytes of information,
or the equivalent of 10 printed bibles,
can be stored on each cartridge. Be-
cause of its ability to compress infor-
mation, the Digital Book System player
can hold two such cartridges at the same
time, allowing easy access to either book
without physically changing the car-
tridge.
